stem: what is changing one requirement, while taking into account competing requirements
explanation: The keyed answer is B: in IEEE 830 terms, consistency is exactly the property that no requirement conflicts with another, so revising one requirement against competing ones is a consistency concern. Note the stem is garbled and the key is disputed: the standard term for a requirement that can be changed cleanly is modifiability, which is not offered, so take B as the exam-expected answer rather than a clean textbook fact.

Panel split three ways with no derivation surviving: first-principles unanswerable (0.3), distractor-analysis B (0.5), skeptic unanswerable (0.8), archaeologist unanswerable (0.8) - skeptic-unanswerable plus sub-0.6 co-judges triggers quarantine. The textbook term the stem actually describes - 'modifiable' (IEEE 830 4.3.6) or 'requirements negotiation' - is not among the options at all, which is the tell that the recall is corrupted. stem: Given a K-map with one 1 circled and the equation is ABCD (ANDed), what is it? (third circle in the first row)
explanation: Answer unverified: this item is quarantined as unanswerable. The K-map figure is missing, and the stem as transcribed makes several options simultaneously true: a circled group whose product keeps all four literals (ABCD) is a single cell, so it is literally a minterm (A); if that cell has no adjacent 1 to merge with, it is also a prime implicant (C) and the only cover of its own minterm, hence an essential prime implicant (D). Without the map you cannot check adjacency, so the intended key cannot be recovered. For revision, learn the definitions: implicant = any product term covering only 1s; prime implicant = an implicant that cannot be enlarged by dropping a literal; essential prime implicant = a prime implicant that is the sole cover of some 1.

stem:
Given a 4x1 mux, what is the operation?
(first input connected by A XOR B, second by B, third and fourth by NOT(B))
Signals are C and D
explanation: Answer unverified: this item is quarantined as unanswerable. The mux figure is missing, so the data-pin order and which of C/D is the select MSB cannot be confirmed. Taking the wiring exactly as transcribed (I0 = A XOR B, I1 = B, I2 = I3 = B', selects C and D), the output works out to F = B'C + A'BC' + AB'D' + BC'D, which is not any named operation (not an adder, comparator or majority gate), and swapping the select order gives a different function. Method to remember: a 4x1 mux output is F = S1'S0'I0 + S1'S0*I1 + S1*S0'I2 + S1*S0*I3, so you substitute each data input and simplify.

stem: Safest way to connect to a secure network through public network is
explanation: Neither TCP nor UDP provides security by itself, so the item is defective as printed; the marked answer is TCP (A). TCP is the defensible pick of the two because it is connection-oriented and is the transport that TLS and most VPN tunnels ride on, while UDP is a bare datagram service. If the real paper offers a VPN or encrypted-tunnel option, that is the correct answer.
quarantineReasons: panel ruling: QUARANTINE. The skeptic rules it unanswerable and two other judges sit below the 0.6 confidence bar (distractor-analysis 0.15, skeptic 0.55-band), which triggers the quarantine rule outright. On substance three judges converge: the stem 'safest way to connect to a secure network through a public network' is the textbook definition of a VPN / encrypted tunnel (IPsec, WireGuard, SSL-TLS), and NEITHER printed option is a security mechanism - TCP (RFC 793) gives ordered reliable delivery with zero confidentiality, integrity or authentication, and UDP (RFC 768) gives less. The 'TCP is least-wrong because TLS rides on TCP' rationalisation does not survive the counter-reading: OpenVPN, IKE/IPsec, WireGuard and DTLS all run over UDP, so the least-wrong argument points at both options depending on which repair you assume - the signature of a broken item. IMPORTANT correction to the other judges' premise: the source-archaeologist rendered page 30 of 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf' and showed the two-option list is NOT a pipeline truncation - the paper itself prints only 'a) TCP b) UDP' with 'a) TCP' bold+underlined. The extraction is faithful; the SOURCE is broken (this KNPC 2016 section is a recalled paper - option 'd) I don't remember' appears verbatim elsewhere). So the item is un-repairable without the real option set and must not be served: shipping it would teach 'TCP = secure', which is false. Study fact to carry instead: safest connection to a private network across a public network = VPN / encrypted tunnel.
no_answer_key: no correct option is marked

stem: Datagram is which layer?
explanation: The key reads 'datagram' as the UDP user datagram, which is the transport-layer PDU. Be aware that most textbooks (Forouzan, Kurose, Tanenbaum) call the network-layer IP PDU a datagram, so 'Network' is the more common answer to this stem and the question is genuinely ambiguous. Learn both: IP datagram at layer 3, UDP datagram at layer 4; no distractors survived, so treat this as a recall card.
quarantineReasons: panel ruling: QUARANTINE. The skeptic rules it unanswerable (0.55) and two other judges are below the 0.6 bar (first-principles 0.5 for 'Transport', distractor-analysis 0.2), triggering the quarantine rule. The source-archaeologist rendered page 31 of 'KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf' and confirmed the extraction is faithful: the paper prints '18) Datagram is which layer?' with a single bold+underlined line 'a) Transport' (item 17 is missing entirely; 15 and 16 are likewise stubs), so the existing no_distractors flag and inline_bold provenance were both correct and the pipeline lost nothing - the RECALLED SOURCE is what is mutilated. That is exactly why the item cannot be keyed: the deciding evidence (was 'Network' on the ballot?) is gone, and both readings are textbook-backed. Bare 'datagram' = the NETWORK-layer PDU in Kurose & Ross, Forouzan and RFC 791 ('internet datagram' = IP); 'user datagram' = the TRANSPORT-layer PDU of UDP (RFC 768) and the OSI PDU tables of the era pair Datagram(UDP) with layer 4 and Packet with layer 3. A single bolded option in a from-memory recall is not strong enough to overturn the dominant textbook convention, and it is equally consistent with mis-remembering. Do not serve as a keyed card. Convert to a two-sided recall fact: IP-flavoured stem -> Network; UDP-flavoured stem -> Transport.

stem: How many types of loops are there in C++?
explanation: This item uses the question-bank taxonomy that counts four loop forms: for, while, do-while, and nested loops, so the expected answer is 4. Be aware the taxonomy is non-standard - a nested loop is just one loop placed inside another, not a distinct statement, and C++11 added the range-based for - but the exam key treats the count as four.
quarantineReasons: panel ruling: QUARANTINED - EXTRACTION ERROR: the item is not in its cited source and its provenance is fabricated. The archaeologist opened the cited file (2.pdf, source originalNumber 26, keyProvenance 'answer_page') and read all 18 pages: it is a KNPC 'Subject 2 - Programming & Algorithm' STUDY PACK - cover, contents, screenshot tutorials of C++ loop syntax, typed data-structures notes, handwritten Big-O working. There is no numbered MCQ list, no question 26, and NO ANSWER PAGE anywhere in the file; a full-text grep for 'types of loop' across every source PDF returns zero hits. So 'answer_page' cannot be true - there is no answer page for it to have come from. The stem, options and explanation are stock text lifted from a generic online C++ quiz and given a false KNPC provenance in content/raw/code-trace.json. Under the panel's first decision rule the archaeologist wins on extraction, and the corrected provenance is 'none': the paper supplies no key, so the bank may not assert one. The item is also pedagogically toxic: its shipped explanation ('while, do while, nested, for') is taxonomically wrong - a nested loop is not a loop type, it is a loop inside a loop. Two judges compiled the four C++11 iteration-statement forms (for, range-based for, while, do-while) and reached the count 4 by a route the source never states, and pre-C++11 the correct count is 3 - so even the number is convention-dependent. Recommend deletion from the bank rather than service. If the candidate ever sees this stem in a practice bank, the bank scores 4; the defensible C++ fact is three classical iteration statements plus range-for.

stem: What are the types of search trees?
answerText: Search trees are ordered trees obeying the search property. Two families: (1) binary search trees - the plain BST plus its self-balancing variants (AVL, red-black, splay, treap); (2) multi-way / m-way search trees - 2-3 tree, 2-3-4 tree, B-tree, B+ tree (used for database and filesystem indexes). The ternary search tree is a specialised string variant.
explanation: Answer unverified: the item ships with no key and is open-ended. The standard taxonomy is: binary search trees (plain BST, plus the self-balancing ones, AVL, red-black, splay, treap) and m-way / multi-way search trees (2-3 tree, B-tree, B+ tree used for database and filesystem indexes), with ternary search trees as a specialized string variant. The organizing property common to all of them is the ordering invariant: keys in the left subtree < node key < keys in the right subtree, generalized to m-1 keys and m children in the multi-way case.

stem: What is the worst-case run-time complexity of binary search algorithm?
explanation: Binary search halves the interval on every comparison, so the worst case is about log2(n) comparisons, i.e. O(log n). O(log n) is not offered here; since big-O is only an upper bound and O(log n) is contained in O(n), O(n) is the tightest of the four choices, which is why D is keyed. Treat this item as a badly worded question, not as evidence that binary search is linear.

stem: What is a DBMS?
answerText: A set of programs to handle databases
explanation: A DBMS is a set of programs that sits between users/applications and the stored data, letting you define, create, store, query, update and administer databases. The database is the data itself; the DBMS is the software that manages it.
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: What is the 2's complement of 1111 1111 1111?
explanation: Invert every bit, then add 1: 1111 1111 1111 -> 0000 0000 0000, then 0000 0000 0000 + 1 = 0000 0000 0001. Check by value: 1111 1111 1111 is -1 in 12-bit two's complement, and -(-1) = +1 = 0000 0000 0001. The classic slip is stopping at the one's complement (all zeros) and forgetting the +1.
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: ARP is it all 1's or 0's?
explanation: An ARP request is broadcast on the LAN, so the Ethernet destination MAC is FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF, which is all 1's, and that is the intended answer. The stem is loose: inside the ARP payload the target hardware address field is filled with all 0's precisely because it is the unknown being asked for. Rule of thumb: the frame's destination is all 1's (broadcast), the unknown field being queried is all 0's.
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: What does a node do in a linked list?
answerText: Each element in a linked list is called a node. A single node contains data and a pointer to the next node which helps in maintaining the structure of the list. The first node is called the head; it points to the first node of the list and helps us access every other element in the list.
explanation: A node is the single element of a linked list: it stores a data field plus a pointer (link) to the next node, and in a doubly linked list also a pointer to the previous node. The chain of next-pointers starting from the head is what holds the list together, which is why a list has no random indexing and must be traversed from the head, giving O(n) access but O(1) insert/delete once you hold the node.

stem: Difference between Moore and Mealy FSM
answerText: Moore depends on present state, Mealy depends on present state and present input.
explanation: The difference is only in the output function: a Moore machine's output depends on the present state alone, while a Mealy machine's output depends on the present state AND the present input. Consequences worth quoting: Mealy usually needs fewer states and reacts in the same cycle as the input, but its outputs can glitch with the inputs; Moore's outputs are stable/registered but appear one clock later. The next-state logic is the same for both (state and input).
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: What is TLB?
answerText: Translation Look Aside Buffer: memory cache that is used to reduce the time taken to access a user memory location.
explanation: TLB = Translation Lookaside Buffer, a small fully/highly associative cache inside the MMU that holds recently used virtual-page-to-physical-frame translations. On a TLB hit the physical address is formed without touching memory; on a miss the hardware or OS walks the page table in memory (slow) and then loads the entry into the TLB. It caches address translations, not data, which is what separates it from the L1/L2 data cache.
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: What is a trap door?
answerText: A computer trapdoor, also known as a back door, provides a secret -- or at least undocumented -- method of gaining access to an application, operating system or online service. Programmers write trapdoors into programs for a variety of reasons.
explanation: A trap door (back door) is a hidden, undocumented entry point deliberately left in a program, OS or service that bypasses the normal authentication and access controls. Programmers add them for debugging or maintenance convenience, and attackers exploit or plant them for persistent access.
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: Definition of propagation delay, rate & throughput
answerText:
Propagation delay: After the packet is transmitted to the transmission medium, it has to go through the medium to reach the destination. Hence the time taken by the last bit of the packet to reach the destination is called propagation delay.
Rate: The speed at which data is transferred within the computer or between a peripheral device and the computer, measured in bytes per second.
Throughput: s a measure of how many units of information a system can process in a given amount of time
explanation: Propagation delay is the time a bit spends travelling through the physical medium, distance / propagation speed, and it depends on link length, not on packet size. Rate (bandwidth) is the link's nominal transfer speed in bits per second, while throughput is the data actually delivered per unit time end to end; throughput is bounded by the slowest link and is normally lower than the rate.
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: What is a collision in hash tables?
answerText: When two keys map to the same value
explanation: A collision is when two different keys hash to the same table slot (index). It is unavoidable once the number of keys exceeds the number of buckets (pigeonhole), so hash tables resolve it with chaining (a list per bucket) or open addressing (probe for another slot).

stem: Mealy and Moore FSM depend on?
answerText:
- Mealy FSM depends on present state and present input
- Moore FSM depends on present state only

A Moore machine's output is a function of the present state only, so it changes only after a clock edge. Consequence: Mealy usually needs fewer states and reacts one cycle earlier, but its output is more prone to glitches.

stem: What is the best time complexity of a Binary Search Tree algorithm?
explanation: Search, insert and delete in a BST follow a single root-to-leaf path with one comparison per level, so the cost is proportional to the height. In the best (balanced) case the height is log2(n), giving O(log n). The classic trap: a BST built from already sorted input degenerates into a linked list of height n, so the worst case is O(n).

stem: How many times will foo(i) work?

stem: What is the truth table for T-flipflop?
answerText:
[T-FF] Truth Table:
T = 0 → Q(t+1) = Q(t)
T = 1 → Q(t+1) = Q(t)'
Equation → Qt+1 = T Qt' + T' Qt
explanation: The T (toggle) flip-flop has one input. T = 0 holds the current state, Q(t+1) = Q(t); T = 1 inverts it, Q(t+1) = Q(t)'. Combining the two rows gives the characteristic equation Q(t+1) = T·Q(t)' + T'·Q(t), which is exactly T XOR Q(t).
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: What is the truth table for JK-flipflop?
answerText:
[JK-FF] Truth Table:
J = 0, K = 0 → Q(t+1) = Q(t)
J = 0, K = 1 → Q(t+1) = 0
J = 1, K = 0 → Q(t+1) = 1
J = 1, K = 1 → Q(t+1) = Q(t)'
Equation → Qt+1 = J Qt' + K' Qt
explanation: The JK flip-flop is the SR flip-flop with the forbidden 1,1 input redefined as toggle. J=0,K=0 holds Q(t); J=0,K=1 resets to 0; J=1,K=0 sets to 1; J=1,K=1 toggles to Q(t)'. Those four rows give the characteristic equation Q(t+1) = J·Q(t)' + K'·Q(t).
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

stem: Binary Numbers, what's the result and overflow of the following?
explanation: 1110 0000 = 224 and 1100 0000 = 192, so 224 + 192 = 416 = 1 1010 0000. The 8-bit result is 1010 0000 with a 1 carried out of the MSB, which is the overflow bit the question is asking for. Note the carry-out signals unsigned overflow only; read as two's complement it is -32 + -64 = -96, which fits in 8 bits, so there is no signed overflow.

stem:
Give an example for each of the following memory addressing modes:
A. Register Direct: ………………………………
B. Immediate: ……………………………………..
C. Register Indirect: …………………………….
D. Register Indirect- Indexed Basic: ………………………
answerText:
A. Move R1,R2 (move R1 to R2)
B. Move #X,R1 (move X to R1)
C. Move (A0),R1 ([A0] → R1)
D. Move X(A0),R1 ([[A0]+X] → R1)
explanation: Each mode differs in how many levels of lookup are needed to reach the operand. Register direct (MOVE R1,R2) uses the register's contents itself; immediate (MOVE #X,R1) carries the literal inside the instruction; register indirect (MOVE (A0),R1) treats the register as a pointer and fetches memory at [A0]; indexed register indirect (MOVE X(A0),R1) adds the displacement X to the base register first, fetching from [A0]+X.
quarantineReasons: free_response: cannot be auto-graded, so it is excluded from quizzes. Browsable here with its model answer.

answerText: See the model-answer figure: the Q waveform is drawn beneath the Clock and T waveforms.
explanation: A T flip-flop toggles Q on each active clock edge only when T = 1, and holds Q unchanged when T = 0. Sample T at the rising edges only: T = 1,0,1,1 with Q starting at 0 gives Q = 1, 1, 0, 1. The trap is treating T as a level (making Q follow T) or reacting to the falling edge.

stem: The truth table of a D flip flop?
answerText: Q(t+1) = D regardless of the present state: Q=0,D=0 -> Q(t+1)=0; Q=0,D=1 -> Q(t+1)=1; Q=1,D=0 -> Q(t+1)=0; Q=1,D=1 -> Q(t+1)=1
explanation: A D flip-flop simply latches its input on the clock edge: Q(t+1) = D, independent of the present state Q. So D=0 gives Q(t+1)=0 and D=1 gives Q(t+1)=1 whether Q was 0 or 1, which is why it is used as a one-bit store or one-clock delay.

stem: What is the subnet mask that gives 510 host to 173.10.0.0?

The trap is forgetting the minus 2 for the network and broadcast addresses, which would push you to /24 (254 hosts, too few). stem: Your company has just been assigned the network 4.0.0.0. How many subnets and hosts-per-subnet you can create with a subnet mask of 255.255.255.0?
explanation: 4.0.0.0 has first octet 4, so it is Class A with a default mask of 255.0.0.0 (/8) = 8 network bits. The given mask 255.255.255.0 is /24, so 24 - 8 = 16 bits are borrowed from the host part, giving 2^16 = 65,536 subnets, and the 32 - 24 = 8 remaining host bits give 2^8 - 2 = 254 usable hosts per subnet (subtracting the network and broadcast addresses). Watch the convention: the -2 applies to HOSTS; older textbooks also subtract 2 from the subnet count (65,534), but the modern count used here is 2^borrowed.

The given mask 255.255.128.0 is 1111 1111.1111 1111.1000 0000.0000 0000 = /17, so only 17 - 16 = 1 bit is borrowed, giving 2^1 = 2 subnets, and the 32 - 17 = 15 remaining host bits give 2^15 - 2 = 32,766 usable hosts per subnet. options.text: Replace the indicated parts, one at a time in the recommended sequence, until the problem is resolved; return unused FRUs to stock

options.label: B
options.text: Replace all of the indicated FRUs at once and return the machine to the customer if the problem is resolved

options.label: C
options.text: Follow the same procedure as in ays replace the system board first if it is on the list of possible FRUs If multiple FRUs are indicated, then software is the most likely source of the problem

explanation: Correct service practice is to swap the FRUs one at a time, in the sequence the maintenance package recommends, retesting after each swap so you learn which part actually failed, and to return the unused parts to stock. Replacing all of them at once may fix the machine but wastes good parts and never isolates the fault.
quarantineReasons: broken_stem: the source paper lost a clause from its own premise - it prints "Suppose that you have a the maintenance package identifies several possible field replaceable units (FRUs)..." (Computer Engineering K-Companies Exam - Questions.pdf, page 17, Q15). The scenario the question asks about is therefore never stated.

fused_options: the same page fuses two distractors into option C ("Follow the same procedure as in ays replace the system board first if it is on the list of possible FRUs If multiple FRUs are indicated, then software is the most likely source of the problem"). Our extraction is character-for-character faithful; reconstructing the missing clause or splitting C would be inventing text.
Repair-pass verdict "quarantine" (content/audit/repair-006.json).

stem: The first computer introduced in Nepal was
explanation: IBM 1401, a second generation computer was brought in Nepal by the Government of Nepal paying One Lakh and twenty five thousands per month to use in the census in 2028 B.S. Before this computer, Nepal was using a calculating device called Facit for statistical tasks.
quarantineReasons: duplicate_options: the source prints c. "IBM 1402" and d. "IBM1402" - the same distractor twice, differing only by a space (Computer Engineering K-Companies Exam - Questions.pdf, page 19, Q8). Key B (IBM 1401) is still uniquely correct, but the duplicate distractor is a source defect and the item is not served.
Repair-pass verdict "quarantine" (content/audit/repair-006.json).

stem: What is the correct answer when using AND gate true table in the digital circuits design:
explanation: An AND gate outputs 1 only when every input is 1, so 1 AND 1 = 1. Note options A and C print the same false claim, and 1 AND 0 = 0, not 1.
quarantineReasons: The source prints two identical options, which makes the item unusable even though the keyed answer is unique.
